title: Wildland Firefighters Congressional Gold Medal Act
short_title: Wildland Firefighters Congressional Gold Medal Act
stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs. (text: CR S231-232)
plain_summary: This bill creates a special gold medal from Congress to honor wildland firefighters. The medal recognizes their brave work fighting dangerous fires to protect America's forests, grasslands, and communities. The gold medal will be put on display at the National Interagency Fire Center for everyone to see. The government can also make and sell bronze copies of the medal to help pay for making it.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs. (text: CR S231-232)

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Wildland Firefighters Congressional Gold Medal Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Findings
sections.text: Congress has learned these important facts. Wildfires have become much worse and more dangerous over the past thirty years. These fires have destroyed homes, roads, bridges, and valuable land owned by the federal government, states, and private people. More than one billion acres of land across the United States could catch fire. About one hundred seventeen million acres of federal land have been identified as having high or very high risk for wildfire. The Forest Service, the Bureau of Land Management, the National Park Service, the United States Fish and Wildlife Service, and the Bureau of Indian Affairs together employ more than eighteen thousand seven hundred wildland firefighters. These firefighters fight wildfires across millions of acres of public and private lands each year. Thousands more workers and volunteers serve as state, local, and contract wildland firefighters. As cities and towns expand into areas near wild lands, wildfires are moving out of remote areas and into neighborhoods across the United States. Wildland firefighters are learning new skills and strategies to fight the growing threat of wildfire in the twenty first century. While protecting people's lives and property remains most important, wildland firefighters also play an important role in taking care of forests properly. Wildland firefighters perform controlled burns and other forest management activities including cutting down trees to keep forests healthy and reduce the risk of catastrophic wildfires. Each wildland firefighter is specially trained to work in changing and extremely dangerous conditions. Wildland firefighters regularly work long days while on a two week work schedule and often sleep in harsh conditions. According to the Forest Service, firefighters generally work sixteen hour days while fighting a fire and they typically work more than two thousand five hundred hours in a six month period. Wildland firefighter crews are emergency responders who handle all types of disasters and use any means necessary to protect life and property while responding to floods, hurricanes, pandemics, and acts of terrorism. Engine and hand crews are the main firefighting workforce and come in different sizes that can be adjusted to fit the specific needs and terrain challenges that each fire presents. Interagency hotshot crews are highly skilled mobile hand crews with expert knowledge about fire suppression tactics. Pilots and aerial fire suppression crews fly air tankers and helicopters to drop water and fire retardant to support decision makers on the ground. Aerially delivered firefighters including helitack crews and smokejumpers exit helicopters and jump from planes into remote and hard to reach areas providing quick and targeted fire suppression and emergency medical rescue. These fire personnel provide oversight and direct action on initial and extended attack incidents. Wildland firefighters in the United States also answer the call to fight wildfires in other countries. During the record setting fires in Australia in two thousand twenty, the United States sent three hundred sixty two firefighters to help. During Canada's historic two thousand twenty three fire season, more than two thousand federal wildland firefighters answered the call. As of the date this law is passed, the United States maintains agreements for mutual help and cooperation for wildland firefighting efforts with Canada, Mexico, Australia, New Zealand, and Portugal. The increases in the severity of wildfires and in the number of active fire season months have also increased the demand for wildland firefighters and related employees. Recruiting and keeping wildland firefighters has been a national problem for many years. Wildland firefighters put their lives on the line to keep the people of the United States safe and some pay the ultimate sacrifice to do so. Between January first two thousand nineteen and January first two thousand twenty five, ninety eight wildland firefighters have lost their lives fighting fires. Immediate and secondary effects from wildfire such as wildfire smoke exposure are directly linked to tens of thousands of firefighter and civilian deaths each year. June thirtieth to July sixth of each year is recognized as a Week of Remembrance to honor the fallen wildland firefighters who sacrificed their lives to protect the wildlands of the United States. National Wildland Firefighter Day is held every year on July second to recognize all who are devoted to wildland firefighting. The excellent efforts of wildland firefighters deserve recognition and it is appropriate and proper to honor those who have previously served as well as current and future firefighters. Wildland firefighters show principles of duty, respect, and integrity in every aspect of service. Each firefighter shows strength, toughness, and determination to protect the forests, grasslands, and communities of the United States. Wildland firefighters do not shy away from dangerous situations but instead risk life and limb to help others. The outstanding accomplishments of these brave individuals continue an unmatched legacy of public service.

sections.title: Section 3 Congressional Gold Medal
sections.text: The Speaker of the House of Representatives and the President pro tempore of the Senate (the person who temporarily leads the Senate when the Vice President is not there) shall make proper arrangements for giving a single gold medal with an appropriate design. This medal will honor wildland firefighters as a group in recognition of their strength, toughness, sacrifice, and service to protect the forests, grasslands, and communities of the United States. For the purpose of giving this medal, the Secretary of the Treasury will create a gold medal with suitable symbols, designs, and words. The Secretary will decide what these should be after talking with the National Interagency Fire Center (the main organization that coordinates wildland firefighting). After the gold medal is presented, it will be given to the National Interagency Fire Center where it will be displayed properly and made available for research. It is Congress's opinion that the National Interagency Fire Center should make sure the medal is displayed and available at appropriate locations, especially locations connected with wildland firefighters.

sections.title: Section 4 Duplicate Medals
sections.text: The Secretary may create and sell copies in bronze of the gold medal created under section three. The price must be enough to cover all costs including labor, materials, dies (tools used to make medals), use of machinery, and overhead expenses.

sections.title: Section 5 Status of Medals
sections.text: The medals created under this law are national medals for purposes of chapter fifty one of title thirty one United States Code (a part of federal law that governs medals). For purposes of sections five thousand one hundred thirty four and five thousand one hundred thirty six of title thirty one United States Code, all medals created under this law shall be considered to be numismatic items (collectible items related to coins and medals).

sections.title: Section 6 Authority to Use Fund Amounts Proceeds of Sale
sections.text: The government is authorized to charge against the United States Mint Public Enterprise Fund whatever amounts are necessary to pay for the costs of the medals created under this law. Money received from the sale of duplicate bronze medals authorized under section four shall be put into the United States Mint Public Enterprise Fund.
